item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Probably something to contact the author directly about. Send them a wikidot PM! :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Unless I'm missing something, shouldn't "geological" in addendum a be geographical instead? It just stood out to me as a bit odd.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So…they're like vampires? They take memories from you and if they take too much you become one?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've only taken a cab once or twice in my life, and this predated Lyft and Uber, but the cabbies were exactly like this, asking increasingly personal questions with an intense desperation. I realize there's a training manual somewhere where it says on paper that people tip more if you establish a rapport first? But there's a thin line between "this guy's easygoing and easy to talk to, I instinctively like him" and "this scumbag is obviously grooming me for a scam of some sort."

Actually, I take that back. The line is not thin at all. It's thick and opaque and concrete, and cabbies are born WAAAAY on the other side of it from normal humanity.

item.title: Re: Author post
item.content:encoded:
This was a lot of fun to read! I had a lot of compassion for SCP-3525-2. I imagine what that experience must be like for him. =(
Bravo, great skip!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To be honest, this is just about what an average Uber drive is like.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Feels very Fifthist, although maybe I'm just inclined o believe that because the listing is a multiple of 25.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Knowledge is an entirely different clusterfuck unto its own.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just out of interest, black cab drivers in London need to pass a test before they can officially drive.

That test is called The Knowledge. Might be something to consider…

Sorry forgot to +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Way to make my anxiety over cab services worse, dude.

When I first started reading, I thought this was going to be another lackluster "recent popular thing but as an scp" entry and was ready to downvote. But at the end of it all, you managed to make a coherent plot, and the whole 'lyft drivers as extradimensional memory-eaters' thing is actually legitimately unnerving.

I might just be saying that because I'm already paranoid, though. Still, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At first glance, I thought the image was loss.
But in all seriousness, I really liked this article.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That was pretty good. I feel like the interview log at the end was making it too obvious about what was going on, but this still had a great sense of mystery and progression in it. Every part here served a purpose while consistently changing things up, which is something I always enjoy in an SCP.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Looks a fair bit meatier than the draft I saw, which is great!

+1 for you

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoy the core idea and logs a lot, it comes together really nicely in a comprehensive way. All I would add is that the tone seems off a bit in the containment procedures, and that perhaps listing what ride sharing app was used in each of the test logs (eg. Test took place in Boston, Lyft was used) and so on. Just to add an additional level of testing competency, which you already did such a good job with.

I very much enjoyed it overall, easily a +1 for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The info of the drivers in question isn't actually stored in the apps' databases, so rating them down doesn't have any effect. Getting a 5-star rating doesn't turn you into one of the drivers; it's the mechanism by which they attract riders. I updated the containment procedures to make it clear that only the "unknown" 5-star drivers are anomalous and to be contained.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just throwing this out there, but couldn't the foundation just rate those guys lower that 5 stars to neutralize them? Also what happens if you work for Uber or whatever and your first customer gives you 5 stars? Do you become one of the drivers?

item.title: Author post
item.content:encoded:
I've used Lyft about a dozen times and every single driver I've gotten was rated 4.7-4.9 stars. I started wondering why I'd never seen a 5-star driver and this SCP was the result.

People who gave feedback on this, in no particular oder:
Forums: Sobellium, Flawed, DrChandra, Zyn
Chat: InitHello, Minmin, Ihp

Image notes (I ran this by the Image Team before posting, this is what they told me to say): The screenshot in the image is from the Lyft app. It was taken by me and modified by me; I added the censor bars, changed the license plate so it didn't actually correspond to any real number, and changed the photo (to a selfie, so no rights issues there). I claim this as transformative fair use.
